result:
**Static**

There's a gas station at the edge of every town I've ever left, and I always notice it on the way out. Never on the way in. I think that's true of most things.

I've been thinking about the word *threshold* lately. How it used to mean the place where you thresh grain, where you beat something against the ground until the useful part separates from the part that's just taking up space. We turned it into a word for doorways. For beginnings. But the original violence is still in there, if you look.

My grandmother kept a bowl of hard candy on her coffee table that nobody was allowed to eat. It was just there. Decorative suffering, my uncle called it once, and she laughed harder than I'd ever seen her laugh, and then composed herself so fast it was like watching a curtain drop. She died with that candy in the bowl. I don't know what happened to it. I should have asked. I'm always not asking the thing I should ask.

There's a specific quality of light at four in the afternoon in October that makes me feel like I've lost something I can't name. Not sad exactly. More like receiving a transmission from a frequency I don't have the equipment to decode. Physicists talk about dark matter as the stuff we know is there because of how everything else behaves around the absence. I think most of what I feel is like that.

I read somewhere that trees in a forest share nutrients through fungal networks underground, that an old tree will feed a young one, that a dying tree will dump its remaining resources into the web before it goes, like someone emptying their pockets before a long trip. Nobody told me that in school. I learned about the Stamp Act. I learned to show my work. Nobody said: here is what generosity looks like at the molecular level. Here is what community looks like before language made it complicated.

The dreams I remember most aren't the dramatic ones. They're the ones where I'm in a house I've never seen but I know it's mine, moving through rooms, feeling a specific texture of ordinary life, and then I wake up and spend a few seconds genuinely grieving a place that doesn't exist. The brain is so committed to the bit. It will construct an entire life for you and then take it back without apology.

I think a lot about all the books that got written and then burned, sunk, lost, pulped, forgotten. All the thoughts that made it all the way to paper and then didn't survive the next century. And then I think about all the thoughts that never made it to paper at all. Someone standing in a field two thousand years ago thinking something true and original about the nature of grief and then going back to work and carrying it alone until they died. That thought is just gone. There's something unbearable about that and also something that makes me feel less alone, knowing that the lost ones are everywhere, that I am probably a lost one too, that most of us are, and we are in good company.

I've started noticing the small sounds in silence. The refrigerator's register. A car three blocks over. The building itself, which settles and breathes like something alive, which maybe it is, in whatever way matters. Silence isn't actually silence. It's just the sounds that didn't make the headline.

When I was eight I found a bird that had flown into our window. It was stunned, not dead, sitting perfectly still in the grass. I cupped it in my hands and felt its heart going at a speed that seemed impossible to maintain. I don't remember what happened next, whether it recovered, whether I set it down and watched it fly, whether something worse happened. The memory ends at the heartbeat. That's what stayed. That small, frantic, perfect pulse against my palms, the whole desperate project of being alive, concentrated and handed to me for a moment before it went back to being something I couldn't hold.

I don't have a conclusion. I've noticed that the pressure to conclude things is one of the ways we lie about experience. Life doesn't conclude, it just continues until it doesn't, and even then we aren't there for the ending. We exit a little early and miss the last few minutes.

The gas station at the edge of town. The candy in the bowl. The book that burned before you got to read it.

The bird's heart, still going.
